title: MazeEval: A Benchmark for Testing Sequential Decision-Making in Language Models
authors: Hafsteinn Einarsson (single author; local extraction header flags candidate/corpus description's 'Einarsson et al.' as a mismatch — the fetched paper lists only one author)
venue: arXiv preprint, cs.AI, v1 submitted 2025-07-27; no separate venue/proceedings identified (header, lines 3-6)
claim: MazeEval isolates pure spatial reasoning in LLMs by having them navigate 5x5 to 15x15 mazes using only (x,y) coordinate feedback and distance-to-wall information via a function-calling move interface, with no visual input. Across 8 SOTA LLMs, OpenAI's O3 achieves perfect navigation up to 30x30 mazes while all other models fail catastrophically beyond roughly 9x9, with 100% of failures caused by excessive looping (revisiting a cell 10+ times). The paper further finds a large English-vs-Icelandic performance gap (models solving mazes 3-4 size-levels smaller in Icelandic), arguing spatial reasoning in LLMs emerges from linguistic training patterns rather than language-agnostic mechanisms (Abstract, lines 26-30).
novelty: The authors present the contribution as: (1) a new coordinate-only, vision-free maze navigation benchmark that 'has [not] been publicly released before,' explicitly designed to be uncontaminated ('ensuring that model providers have not had the opportunity to train their models on similar data,' Introduction, line 44); (2) isolating spatial reasoning from visual processing and from richly-described environments, unlike ALFWorld/VirtualHome/TextWorld which 'assume rich visual or textual descriptions' (Related Work 2.1-2.2, lines 62, 66-68); (3) the first (per the paper) systematic cross-linguistic test of whether LLM spatial reasoning transfers to a lower-resource, morphologically rich language (Icelandic) using identical maze configurations across languages (3.4, lines 113-117); (4) a function-calling interaction interface intended to remove NL-parsing ambiguity and mirror real agent deployments (3.3, lines 105-107).

protocol.description: This is a strictly multi-step, closed-loop interactive protocol, not single-shot. Models call a `move(direction)` function repeatedly; each call returns updated state, and the model must decide the next move based on accumulating history. Termination occurs on reaching the goal, revisiting any cell 10+ times, or exceeding a 3n^2 move budget for an n x n maze (Methods 3.3, lines 107-111).
protocol.observation_content: Per Appendix A's literal prompt example (lines 296-323), at each step the model is given: (1) a restated coordinate-system explanation (X rightward=east, Y downward=south); (2) distance-to-wall in each of 4 cardinal directions, e.g. 'North: 2 cells (to cell (0, 2))... East: 0 cells... West: 0 cells'; (3) current position as (x,y); (4) the goal position as (x,y); (5) a full travel history line-by-line, each entry showing position, the distances observed there, and the action taken, e.g. '1. At (0, 0) [saw: 1E, 0W, 0S, 0N] - Moved east to position (1, 0)'; (6) an explicit warning about the 10-visit loop-termination rule. Section 3.3 (lines 108-109) confirms this programmatically: 'For each step, the model receives four key pieces of information: its current position coordinates..., the distance to walls in each cardinal direction..., the goal position coordinates..., and a complete history of previously visited positions along with the visibility distances that were available at each of those positions.' There is NO full/global grid rendering shown to the model at any point — this is a partial-observation protocol; the model only ever sees local wall-distance readings plus its own coordinate and cumulative history, never the maze's global layout or unexplored regions (3.2, lines 92-103).

representations.summary: The paper does not run a comparative ablation across representation formats (e.g., ASCII grid vs adjacency list vs coordinate list vs image); it uses exactly one fixed representation throughout — numerical (x,y) coordinates plus per-direction distance-to-wall counts, delivered via function-calling, explicitly to eliminate visual/map input ('This configuration eliminates visual reasoning entirely, focusing purely on spatial reasoning through numerical coordinates,' 3.2 line 103). The only cross-condition comparison performed is linguistic (English vs Icelandic prompt/direction-name translation, 3.4), not representational. Field marked unverified for any grid-image/ASCII-map/adjacency-list comparison: unverified — missing from extraction (no such ablation appears to exist in this paper; Discussion 5.4 lists 'investigating whether performance improves with visual input or richer environmental descriptions' and 'visualization-of-thought prompting' as future work, not something tested here, lines 258).

models.name: OpenAI O3
models.strategies: unverified — missing from extraction (no CoT/few-shot/ReAct prompting variants are described; single function-calling protocol applied uniformly to all 8 models per 3.3, 3.6)
models.key_results: Perfect navigation success across all maze sizes tested in both languages, the only model to do so (4.1, lines 148-149; Table 1, lines 168-178, shows '15+' for both English and Icelandic). Additional unstructured testing found it solved a 30x30 maze in both languages but failed a 40x40 maze in both languages (4.3, line 184). Near-perfect efficiency 0.89-1.0 across all sizes/languages (4.4, line 196). Virtually no wall hits and minimal backtracking (4.5, line 210).

models.name: Claude Opus 4
models.key_results: 'Strong performer,' robust to 8x8/9x9 before degrading (4.2, line 157). Table 1: max maze size English=11, Icelandic=8 (lines 171). Backtracking rose from 6.8 average in 5x5 mazes to 68.6 in 12x12 mazes (English) (4.5, line 210). Individual language-effect test: p=0.016, Cohen's d=0.96 (pre-Bonferroni-correction significant) (4.3, line 186).

models.name: Claude Sonnet 4
models.key_results: Strong performer tier (4.2, line 157). Table 1: max size English=12, Icelandic=8, one of the largest English-Icelandic drops reported (lines 172, 180). Individual language effect: p=0.047, d=0.57 (4.3, line 186).

models.name: Gemini 2.5 Pro
models.key_results: Strong performer tier (4.2, line 157). Table 1: max size English=11, Icelandic=8 (line 174). Individual language effect: p=0.008, d=1.20, largest individual effect size reported (4.3, line 186).

models.name: Gemini 2.5 Flash
models.key_results: 'Weaker performer,' struggles beyond 7x7, fails completely at 9x9+ (4.2, line 159). Table 1: max size English=8, Icelandic=8 (line 173). Erratic wall-collision spike to 17.2 average hits in 9x9 Icelandic mazes (4.5, line 210).

models.name: GPT-4o
models.key_results: Weaker performer tier (4.2, line 159). Table 1: max size English=8, Icelandic=8 (line 175).

models.name: GPT-4o-mini
models.key_results: Table 1 lists '—' / '—' for both languages (line 176), i.e., data not reported/available for max solvable size in the table; text (4.2) groups it among 'weaker performers' failing beyond 7x7.

models.name: GPT-4.1-mini
models.key_results: Weaker performer tier (4.2, line 159). Table 1: max size English=8, Icelandic=7 (line 177).

headline_numbers: O3 achieves perfect success across all maze sizes tested (5x5-15x15, plus an ad hoc 30x30) in both English and Icelandic; fails at 40x40 (Abstract line 30; 4.3 line 184; Table 1).
100% of all failures (across all other 7 models, both languages) are attributed to excessive looping — revisiting a cell 10+ times (Abstract line 30; 4.5 line 204; 5.3 line 240).

Sharp performance-tier cutoff around 9x9: 'Strong Performers' (Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro) hold up to 8x8/9x9; 'Weaker Performers' (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash) fail beyond 7x7, mostly failing completely at 9x9+ (4.2, lines 153-161).

Models solve mazes 3-4 size-levels smaller in Icelandic than English on average (Abstract line 30; Conclusion line 282); aggregate Wilcoxon signed-rank test across all models: W=273, p<0.001, Cohen's d=0.50 (4.3, line 186).

Individual per-model language effects before Bonferroni correction: Claude Opus 4 p=0.016/d=0.96; Claude Sonnet 4 p=0.047/d=0.57; Gemini 2.5 Pro p=0.008/d=1.20; only the aggregate comparison survives Bonferroni correction at alpha=0.00625 (4.3, lines 186-188).

Navigation efficiency (optimal path length / actual steps) for O3: 0.89-1.0 across all sizes/languages; other models: 0.7-1.0 on small mazes dropping to 0.4-0.7 on larger mazes among successful runs only (4.4, lines 196-198).
Wall-collision rate is low even in failed runs, averaging 0.8 hits, showing models correctly use distance feedback for basic movement even when they fail overall (4.5, line 208; 5.3, line 246).

Backtracking scales sharply with size for weaker models: e.g. Claude Opus 4 rises from 6.8 avg backtracks at 5x5 to 68.6 at 12x12 in English (4.5, line 210); some models average over 80 backtracks versus an optimal path of only 20-30 steps (Appendix B, line 335).
failure_modes: Universal/singular failure mode: 100% of failures across all non-O3 models and both languages are due to revisiting the same cell 10+ times before the hard termination cap — described as 'excessive cell visits' / looping (4.5 line 204; 5.3 line 240).

No failures were due to exhausting the 3n^2 move budget — models get trapped in loops well before running out of moves, meaning the loop-termination cap (not the step budget) is the binding constraint (4.5, line 206).
Backtracking (revisits of previously visited cells) escalates steeply with maze size and is treated as the precursor/mechanism behind the ultimate looping failure (4.5, lines 208-212; Appendix B).

Wall collisions (attempting to move through a wall) are comparatively rare/minor (~0.8 avg even in failed runs), so the dominant failure is state/memory management (losing track of where one has been) rather than misreading the distance-to-wall signal (4.5 line 208; 5.3 line 246).
Failure is systematic rather than stochastic: 'The consistency of these failure points across multiple runs reinforces that these limitations are systematic rather than stochastic' (4.2, line 161).

Interpreted mechanistically as models 'treat[ing] each navigation decision as relatively independent,' i.e., failing to integrate travel history into a persistent spatial memory/mental map despite that full history being provided in-context every turn (5.3, lines 240-244).
Degradation is worse in Icelandic than English for the same models/mazes, suggesting the spatial-memory failure is compounded by (or partly caused by) weaker linguistic grounding (4.5 line 212; 5.2).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper does not test or claim anything about a genuine single-forward-pass / one-shot prediction condition — every evaluated condition is the same interactive, multi-step function-calling loop with full move history re-supplied each turn (3.3). Its findings are entirely about behavior across an extended interaction: the central conclusion is that models can correctly use *local* single-step information (wall-collision rate is low, meaning per-step distance-to-wall reasoning is basically intact) but fail to *integrate that information over the interaction* into a persistent global spatial state, which is why the failure mode is 100% looping rather than one-off errors. The paper does not provide any data point that isolates 'can a model predict one derived fact (e.g., final coordinate) directly from a fully-specified initial map+command sequence, in a single shot' — that specific claim/condition is unverified — missing from extraction (not tested; the closest is O3's near-perfect step-efficiency, which is still measured across the interactive trajectory, not a single derived-fact prediction). Discussion 5.4 explicitly flags 'testing with partial map revelation or memory of previously seen areas could bridge the gap between minimal feedback and full visibility conditions' as unaddressed future work, and 5.1/5.4 note 'visualization-of-thought' (i.e., giving models a rendered map) as a proposed but untested future direction — confirming full-observation and single-shot conditions are outside this paper's scope.

relation_to_candidate.quadrant: Partial-observation x Interactive (multi-step). MazeEval sits in the opposite quadrant from c19 on both axes: c19 is full-observation (entire ASCII grid shown at once) and single-shot (one prediction call against an oracle), whereas MazeEval is partial-observation (only local wall-distances + own coordinate + cumulative history, never the map) and interactive (a closed action loop with per-step function calls, terminating on loop-detection or budget).
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are deterministic, seeded, coordinate-based grid-navigation tasks with programmatic/oracle-verifiable ground truth, evaluated via exact/derived-fact correctness rather than free-form generation, and both are explicitly designed to be contamination-resistant via novel/regenerated instances rather than reused public datasets (MazeEval Introduction line 44; c19 'randomized grids are contamination-proof'). Both use cardinal-direction movement primitives (north/south/east/west or turn/forward) and both examine model correctness against a small ground-truth simulator/oracle. MazeEval's core empirical finding — that most models handle small grids fine but collapse above roughly 9x9 due to failure to integrate accumulating state (looping) — is presented in c19's own related-work note as 'directly probing the grid state-prediction floor c19 depends on,' i.e., c19 treats MazeEval's interactive-loop failure as evidence that even the underlying single-fact state-tracking primitive (which c19 isolates into one shot) is nontrivial for models at scale.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Observability: MazeEval never shows the model a full map — it must build any global representation purely from a textual history of local readings; c19 shows the complete grid, walls, and robot pose in one rendering. (2) Interaction structure: MazeEval requires up to O(n^2) sequential function calls with state carried by the harness between calls; c19 is exactly one model call producing one token, with no environment loop, no move budget, and no loop-termination logic (there is nothing to loop through in c19). (3) Output target: MazeEval's 'success' is reaching a goal cell through a sequence of chosen actions (many valid paths could count if efficiency were ignored, though the paper does track path efficiency); c19 asks for exactly one derived fact (final coordinate/heading/carrying-flag) from a *given, fixed* command string, removing action-choice/path-planning entirely — there is no exploration or decision-making in c19, only deterministic simulation-following. (4) Confound structure: MazeEval's dominant reported failure (looping from lost spatial memory across many turns) is structurally impossible to observe in c19's single-shot design, since there are no repeated turns to lose track of. (5) MazeEval adds a cross-linguistic (English/Icelandic) axis c19 does not test. (6) MazeEval uses real wall/maze topology requiring pathfinding; c19 uses simpler small grids (~6x6) with invented commands/wrap-clamp conventions rather than complex maze topology as the source of difficulty.
relation_to_candidate.what_c19_would_add: c19 would isolate exactly the sub-capability MazeEval's Discussion (5.4) flags as unexplored: whether a model can correctly simulate/derive a single final state fact when the *entire* problem (map + full command sequence) is given up front in one shot, with no requirement to plan a path, choose actions, or maintain state across a long multi-turn loop. Since MazeEval shows models keep local per-step distance-feedback processing largely intact (low wall-collision rate) but fail at integrating information *across many sequential turns* (100% looping failures), c19 would test whether that integration failure is really about long-horizon interactive state accumulation specifically, or whether it also shows up when the model must run the exact same number of state-transitions internally within a single forward pass (no interactive scaffolding, no chance to re-read history line by line as separately supplied context). c19 additionally introduces invented commands and wrap-vs-clamp rule variation that MazeEval's fixed, real-wall/DFS-maze design does not test, probing rule-following/convention-application rather than pure multi-step memory. Because MazeEval's harness re-supplies the full travel history verbatim every turn (functionally close to 'full observation of the past' though never of the unexplored map), c19's single-shot, fully-observed variant would clarify whether removing the turn-by-turn interaction loop (while keeping equivalent information content) raises or preserves the failure rate MazeEval attributes to spatial-memory limitations.

corpus_claim: MazeEval is coordinate-based maze navigation with distance-to-wall feedback; models fail beyond nine-by-nine mazes via looping, directly probing the grid state-prediction floor c19 depends on. (Corpus cites 'Einarsson et al.' — acquisition found it is single-author.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The factual/empirical core is supported verbatim by the paper: it is indeed coordinate-based maze navigation with distance-to-wall feedback (3.2, lines 92-103), and it reports a sharp failure threshold around 9x9 with 100% of failures caused by looping/excessive revisits (4.2 line 161; 4.5 line 204). The parenthetical citation correction is also confirmed correct by this extraction: the paper's header lists a single author, 'Hafsteinn Einarsson,' not multiple authors ('Einarsson et al.'), matching the acquisition note (header lines 4, 9-17; byline line 22). The one imprecision in the corpus claim as written is characterizing the finding as 'models fail beyond nine-by-nine mazes' universally — the paper's own tiering (4.2) shows this threshold applies mainly to the 'weaker performers' (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash, failing beyond 7x7-9x9) and partially to 'strong performers' (Claude Opus 4/Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro, holding to 8x8/9x9 before degrading, with Claude Sonnet 4 actually reaching size 12 in English per Table 1) — while O3 is a clean exception solving up to 30x30. So '9x9' is a reasonable single-number summary of the modal/most-common failure threshold but is not literally true of all 8 models, hence partially-supported rather than fully supported.
baseline_relevant: Grid sizes actually used: 5x5 through 15x15 for the main 8-model x 2-language comparison (5 unique seeded mazes generated per size, DFS-guaranteed single solution path), plus an unreplicated extra data point of 30x30 (solved by O3) and 40x40 (failed by O3) (Methods 3.1 lines 84-90; Results 4.3 line 184).

Prompt format that matters: the exact per-turn prompt in Appendix A (lines 300-323) is the only format tested — restates coordinate-system conventions, gives 4-directional wall-distances, current position, goal position, full annotated travel history, and an explicit warning about the 10-visit loop-termination rule. No ablation of alternative formats (e.g., full-map ASCII, adjacency list) is reported in this paper.

Metrics used: success rate (% mazes solved within step limit), step efficiency (optimal path length / actual steps, conditioned on success), wall-hit count, and backtrack/revisit count (3.5, lines 119-123).

Hard constraints imposed to bound runaway interaction: max 10 visits to any single cell (else terminate as failure) and max total moves = 3n^2 for an n x n maze (3.3, line 111) — useful bounding pattern if c19 or related interactive baselines need failure-termination rules, though c19 itself is single-shot and would not need a loop cap.

Observed floor/ceiling: O3 is a clean ceiling case (100% success up to 30x30, near-perfect 0.89-1.0 efficiency, near-zero wall hits/backtracks) while the other 7 models show a floor collapsing to near-zero success above ~7x7 to 9x9 depending on model tier (4.1-4.2, 4.4-4.5); this establishes that grid/maze state-tracking difficulty is highly model-dependent and not a universal floor — relevant for calibrating c19's naive-prompt floor risk noted in its own Verdict section.

Contamination/data-release status: the authors explicitly designed the benchmark to be unpublished/novel to avoid training contamination — 'No dataset of this specific type... has been publicly released before, ensuring that model providers have not had the opportunity to train their models on similar data' (Introduction, line 44) — directly supporting c19's own contamination-resistance design goal (randomized/seeded grids).

Multilingual finding relevant to baseline design: identical maze configurations were reused across English and Icelandic to isolate the language variable (3.4, line 115); if c19 baseline work considers language variation as a stratum, MazeEval's approach (same seeded instances, translated prompts/direction tokens) is a directly reusable pattern.

The paper's own Limitations (5.4) flag exactly the gap c19 fills: it never tests full/partial map revelation, richer environmental description, or visualization-of-thought (rendered-map) prompting — all listed as unexplored future directions, confirming this specific full-observation/single-shot space is open and not covered by MazeEval itself (5.4, lines 258-260).
quotes: "No dataset of this specific type, namely coordinate-based maze navigation with distance-to-wall feedback, has been publicly released before, ensuring that model providers have not had the opportunity to train their models on similar data motivated by public datasets." (Introduction, line 44)

"For each step, the model receives four key pieces of information: its current position coordinates in the maze, the distance to walls in each cardinal direction, the goal position coordinates it needs to reach, and a complete history of previously visited positions along with the visibility distances that were available at each of those positions." (Methods 3.3, lines 108-109)

"To prevent infinite loops and ensure fair comparison, two constraints are imposed: each model can visit any cell at most ten times, and the total number of moves is limited to 3n² for an n×n maze." (Methods 3.3, line 111)
"OpenAI's O3 model demonstrates exceptional performance, achieving perfect navigation success across all maze sizes in both languages, making it the only model to do so." (Results 4.1, lines 148-149)
"100% of failures were due to excessive cell visits, where models revisited the same cell 10 or more times before termination." (Results 4.5, line 204)

"Notably, no failures occurred due to exceeding the movement budget (3n² steps for n×n mazes), indicating that models consistently became trapped in loops well before exhausting their step allocation." (Results 4.5, line 206)
"models performed significantly better in English than Icelandic (W=273, p<0.001, Cohen's d=0.50)" (Results 4.3, line 186)
"This configuration eliminates visual reasoning entirely, focusing purely on spatial reasoning through numerical coordinates." (Methods 3.2, line 103)

corrections: baseline_relevant[4]: typo '~7x9 to 9x9' corrected to '~7x7 to 9x9' (source describes weaker performers struggling 'beyond 7x7 mazes' and strong performers holding to '8x8 or 9x9'; '7x9' did not correspond to any maze size discussed in the paper).
